Sap

Artificial Intelligence

Data Integration

Apache Kafka

Onibex

10 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

If you’ve ever tried to get real-time data out of SAP, you already know the feeling. You open a transaction, you see the data you need (a sales order, a billing document, a material master) and then someone asks you to stream it into Kafka. Continuously. Without ABAP. Without breaking the SAP system. Without a team of consultants.
That feeling is the problem One Connect was built to solve.
This article walks through exactly how One Connect works, from the raw SAP table layer all the way to a live Kafka topic with an enforced Avro schema. I’ll go technically deep, but I’ll keep the concepts grounded so you can follow along whether you’re an SAP architect, a data engineer, or a technical lead trying to understand what your team is actually deploying.

First, Why SAP Data Is So Hard to Stream
SAP is the system of record for some of the most valuable data in any enterprise: financials, procurement, inventory, sales. The problem isn’t that the data isn’t there. It’s that SAP was designed for transactions, not analytics. And definitely not for streaming.
Here’s what I mean. A single sales order in SAP doesn’t live in one place. It’s split across at least two tables: VBAK (the order header: customer, document date, total value) and VBAP (the order line items: materials, quantities, prices). To get a complete “Sales Order” as a meaningful business object, you need to know that relationship, write the join, and keep it in sync with any schema changes SAP rolls out.
Now multiply that across a full ERP system. Financial postings split between BKPF and BSEG. Billing documents across VBRK and VBRP. Material masters across MARA, MARC, MARD. Each of those relationships has business rules, dependent fields, and potential custom extensions your team built years ago as Z-tables.
Traditional approaches to this problem usually go one of two ways:

• Batch ETL: a nightly job that pulls everything and lands it in a warehouse by morning. It works, until real-time decisions depend on data that’s 18 hours old.

• Custom ABAP extraction: bespoke code written by SAP developers that pulls data on a schedule or trigger. It works too, until the ABAP developer leaves or SAP releases a patch.
Both approaches leave you with the same outcome: stale data, brittle pipelines, and a team that’s too busy maintaining extractions to build anything with the data.
One Connect takes a different approach entirely.

What One Connect Is
One Connect is an SAP-verified, real-time data streaming platform built by Onibex. It sits between your SAP system and your Kafka cluster (specifically Confluent) and handles the full journey from raw SAP tables to clean, schema-governed Kafka topics.
It’s made up of two components that each handle a different part of the problem:

• The SAP Data Modeler: the modeling layer, where you define what your data should look like as a business object

• The Smart Gateway: the delivery layer, where that modeled data gets serialized and streamed into Kafka
Think of it like this:
SAP ECC / S/4HANA
↓
Data Modeler ← Onibex Marketplace (150+ pre-packaged Data Products)
↓
Smart Gateway → Avro serialization + Schema Registry
↓
Confluent Kafka Topic
↓
Any destination: Databricks, Snowflake, watsonx, Power BI, etc.Let’s walk through each layer.

The Data Modeler: Turning Raw Tables Into Data Products
The Data Modeler is where you tell One Connect what you actually want. Instead of writing SQL joins or ABAP function modules, you work in a drag-and-drop interface to build what Onibex calls Data Products: clean, governed, stream-ready versions of your SAP business entities.
You may hear the terms Business Object and Data Product used interchangeably, but they are related and distinct. A Business Object is SAP’s internal representation of a real-world entity, structured as nodes and behaviors, living inside SAP. A Data Product is what One Connect produces from it: a structured, consumable version of that same entity, ready for streaming. One Connect bridges the two.
Starting With the Marketplace
One Connect ships with 150+ pre-packaged SAP Data Products in the Onibex Marketplace. These cover the most common SAP business objects out of the box: Sales Orders, Billing Documents, Purchase Orders, Material Masters, Customers, Vendors, and more.
When you open the Marketplace and select the Billing Document entity, for example, it already knows:

• The header table is VBRK

• The line item table is VBRP

• The accounting document header is BKPF

• The accounting line items are BSEG

• The join keys between them

• Which fields to expose as columns
You didn’t write that. That mapping was pre-built following SAP best practices, and it’s ready to stream the moment you activate it.
Adding Your Own Tables
Not everything you need will be in the Marketplace. SAP systems at scale usually have years of customization: Z-tables (custom tables your team built, identifiable by a Z prefix), non-standard CDS views, or additional standard tables you want to append to an existing entity.
The Data Modeler handles all three through the same interface. You click Insert Table, search for your table or CDS view by name, and the tool brings in the structure. You can then:

• Configure the join to the existing entity (manually or automatically, depending on the relationship)

• Select which columns to include downstream

• Define filters at the SAP application layer, so only the data you want ever leaves the SAP system
This last point matters more than it might seem. Filtering at the source rather than at the destination means you’re not moving data you don’t need across a network. For large-volume tables like BSEG (which can hold billions of rows in mature SAP systems), this is the difference between a performant pipeline and one that never finishes loading.
CDS Views and What They Add
CDS (Core Data Services) views are SAP’s own abstraction layer on top of raw tables. They’re virtual structures (think of them as named, reusable SQL views built into the SAP ABAP stack) that surface business-friendly field names and pre-computed relationships.
If your SAP team has built CDS views for their key entities, you can pull those directly into the Data Modeler. One Connect treats them exactly like standard tables. This means if someone has already done the work of defining a clean “Sales Order” structure in CDS, you don’t have to redo it in the Modeler. You just reference the view.
Triggering Events: How One Connect Knows When Something Changed
Real-time streaming requires knowing when a record in SAP has changed. In SAP, every meaningful business action (a sales order posted, a billing document created, a payment cleared) fires what is called a Business Event. One Connect listens for those Business Events natively, without polling and without custom ABAP code.
The underlying framework that delivers the Business Event depends on your SAP version. On ECC and hybrid environments, Business Events are delivered via the BOR (Business Object Repository): an object-oriented event model that fires on business object state changes. For the Billing Document entity, for example, this is configured as EventDAct on the BILLING change document object. The moment a billing document is created, updated, or deleted in SAP, that event fires and One Connect streams the delta downstream instantly.
On S/4HANA, Business Events are delivered via RAP (RESTful Application Programming Model): SAP’s modern, cloud-ready eventing framework built natively for S/4HANA. One Connect supports both.
Beyond BOR and RAP, One Connect also supports BTE (Business Transaction Events), PPF (Post Processing Framework), Change Pointers, and BW Extractors for initial historical load. Every SAP version and every business scenario is covered. No scheduler. No polling. Just the event firing the moment the action happens in SAP.

The Smart Gateway: From SAP to Kafka, Cleanly
Once the Data Modeler has defined your entities and their triggering events, the Smart Gateway is what actually moves the data. This is the component that handles serialization, schema management, and the physical delivery into Confluent.
What the Smart Gateway Does in Four Steps
1. Receives the delta from SAP
When a Business Event fires in SAP, One Connect captures the changed record and passes it to the Smart Gateway. This includes both inserts (new records) and updates (changed records).
2. Serializes to Avro
Rather than sending raw JSON or CSV, the Smart Gateway serializes every record into Avro format. Avro is a compact binary serialization format that enforces a strict schema at write time. Every field has a defined type. Every record is validated before it gets written to Kafka.
Why does this matter? Because JSON is flexible in a way that causes problems at scale. A field can be a string in one record and null in the next. A column can be renamed in SAP and your consumer finds out the hard way. Avro stops that from happening by enforcing the contract at the producer side.
3. Creates and manages schemas in the Confluent Schema Registry
Before writing to Kafka, the Smart Gateway registers the schema for each Data Product in the Confluent Schema Registry. This is a centralized repository for all the schemas your Kafka topics use, and it’s what allows consumers downstream to know exactly what they’re receiving.
One Connect handles this automatically. You don’t need to write schema definitions manually or configure the registry yourself. When a new Data Product is activated, One Connect creates the corresponding schema in the registry on the fly.
4. Creates Kafka topics and writes records
The Smart Gateway also creates the Kafka topic for each Data Product automatically, naming it consistently based on the entity. From that point forward, every change to that SAP object writes a new record to the topic in Avro format, with the schema enforced by the registry.
Schema Evolution: What Happens When SAP Changes
One of the most painful parts of maintaining SAP extractions is schema evolution. SAP releases patches, your team adds fields to custom tables, or a CDS view gets extended. In a traditional pipeline, any of those changes can break downstream consumers.
One Connect handles schema evolution through the Confluent Schema Registry’s compatibility rules. When a schema changes (say, a new column is added to an entity) the Smart Gateway registers the updated schema as a new version. Consumers that haven’t updated yet continue reading records using the previous schema version. When they’re ready, they upgrade to the new version.
This is schema evolution done right: backward-compatible by default, with the registry as the single source of truth.
Network Architecture: How the Connection Works
One Connect runs as a SaaS deployment on Azure. The Smart Gateway communicates outbound from your SAP system to the Onibex Cloud, then onward to your Confluent Cloud or Confluent Platform cluster.
A key security point: no inbound firewall rules are required on your SAP system. The connection is outbound-only, which means your security team doesn’t need to open ports into a production SAP environment. The Smart Gateway initiates the connection. SAP never receives one.

The Medallion Architecture in Practice
If you work in data engineering, you’re probably familiar with the medallion architecture: the Bronze/Silver/Gold pattern for organizing data by quality and readiness. One Connect operationalizes this pattern specifically for SAP data, which is worth making explicit.
Bronze: Raw SAP Data
The Bronze layer is the raw extraction. Every SAP table that feeds into your Data Products is captured exactly as it exists in the source system: BKPF, BSEG, VBAK, VBAP, MARA, EKKO, and whatever custom tables your team has built. No transformation, no aggregation. Just the source data, preserved as-is.
Silver:
